summary: The post argues that WebSockets, while designed for browser-to-server bidirectional communication, are far more complex than most developers expect. Ronacher explains that much of this complexity stems from security constraints and broken HTTP middleware in the real world. He contends that WebSockets are a poor fit when neither endpoint is a browser, and that traditional TCP or alternative mechanisms can be simpler and more reliable. A major practical conclusion is to always use TLS because it avoids proxy breakage and reduces protocol hacks like masking. He ultimately sees WebSockets as an awkward, evolving standard that developers must live with, but not one to reach for casually.
key_points: WebSockets add a heavy, message-framed protocol on top of TCP, largely to accommodate browser and proxy constraints.
The real-world network environment (proxies, ELBs, mobile networks) makes unencrypted WebSockets unreliable; TLS is effectively required.
Handshake, framing, masking, and fragmentation make server implementations complex and often partially compliant.
Authorization and client IP handling are harder with TCP-level proxies, pushing custom token-based schemes.
WebSocket URL schemes and browser differences introduce additional friction and incompatibilities.
money_quotes: The amount of shitty HTTP middleware installed all around the world is just staggering.
Not encrypting your connection is definitely something you will regret sooner or later.
The reason why websockets have a separate URL is beyond me.
Masking is the best part because it makes debugging so incredible fun.
Maybe a simple policy file like Unity3D and Flash are using would have been a better idea and just let people speak TCP themselves.
key_insight: WebSockets are a necessary but messy compromise for browser communication, and in practice you should treat them as complex, TLS-required, and not a general replacement for TCP.
